teams.bio: The Cowboys are the league's boom-or-bust franchise — two championships (2022, 2024) but a losing overall record, with Jose Ramirez single-handedly carrying the lineup through every season. The highs are as high as it gets: a dominant 17-5 run in 2024 followed by the trophy. The lows are equally dramatic: a 4-18 collapse in 2025 sent them to the Saccko bracket just months after defending their title. But 2026 looks like a title year again. Ian's squad has stormed out to a 4-0 start and posted the league's highest point total through the first four weeks — and the most striking part is that Jose Ramirez is barely in the conversation. This is no longer a one-star team.
teams.strengths: The Cowboys have quietly assembled the deepest roster in the league. Yordan Alvarez has been the most dominant hitter of the young season, and Ben Rice, James Wood, and Oneil Cruz are all contributing at a level that would lead other teams. Max Fried and Cam Schlittler have both been elite at the top of the rotation, giving Ian a genuine 1-2 punch. Two titles in four years is no accident — this franchise knows how to build a team that peaks at the right moment, and right now it has more firepower than ever.
teams.weaknesses: The injury list is the thing that should keep Ian up at night. Jackson Chourio hasn't played, Shane Bieber and Blake Snell are both zeros, and Bryan Abreu is in negative territory. The back of this roster relies on some speculative arms that could crumble if the depth guys don't deliver. The Cowboys' history of extreme swings — from 17-5 to Saccko in a single offseason — suggests that this team is only a few key injuries away from another collapse, and the early-season casualty list is already testing that theory.

teams.bio: The Chinook carry the most frustrating stat in the league: a winning 45-40 all-time record with just one playoff appearance to show for it. Freddie Freeman has been their most consistent weapon over the years, and 2023 — their sole postseason appearance — showed what this team can do when everything clicks. In 2026, the question of whether Owen finally breaks through is looking like a genuine yes. Portland sits at 3-1 with the second-highest point total in the league and a roster so deep it borders on absurd.
teams.strengths: The Chinook may have the most complete roster in the league right now. Seventeen different players have put up meaningful point totals through the first four weeks — there is no position of true weakness. Matt Olson, Ozzie Albies, Drake Baldwin, and Randy Arozarena are all producing at star levels, and Will Warren, George Kirby, and Jacob Misiorowski form the kind of three-deep rotation other managers dream about. Freddie Freeman is still contributing. This team doesn't need to rely on anyone having a monster week because it wins with sustained production across the entire lineup.
teams.weaknesses: Portland's greatest enemy is its own identity. With no true first-round superstar anchor, the ceiling on any given week is lower than what the Cowboys or Emus can put up, which means the Chinook need to win ugly — accumulating advantages across the lineup rather than dominating a matchup. Carlos Rodon has been a zero so far and Jackson Holliday hasn't contributed, which means two roster spots are already functioning as dead weight. If the depth players cool off simultaneously, Owen's team could slip faster than the standings suggest.

teams.bio: The Pepperoni Rolls came out of the gate hot — back-to-back playoff appearances in 2022 and 2023 had this squad looking like a perennial contender. But a steep fall followed, with a 5-17 record and Saccko bracket finish in 2025 raising serious questions about the rebuild. The 2026 draft was supposed to be the reset, but so far the early returns are mixed: a 1-3 start despite a roster loaded with recognizable names suggests the talent hasn't fully translated yet.
teams.strengths: On paper, this is one of the most talented rosters in the league. Elly De La Cruz, Christian Walker, and Mike Trout in the same lineup is a lot to handle, and Paul Skenes gives Douglas a genuine frontline ace who can anchor the staff for years. JJ Wetherholt has emerged as a legitimate secondary bat, and Michael Harris II and Otto Lopez add the kind of quality depth you can build around. The ceiling on this team is real — it just hasn't been reached yet.
teams.weaknesses: Julio Rodriguez — supposedly the franchise centerpiece — has been a non-factor through the early weeks, which is exactly the structural risk that plagued this team in 2025. The rotation behind Skenes is shaky: Shane McClanahan, Eury Perez, and Jesus Luzardo are all question marks who have underperformed so far. The bullpen is patched together from scrap. When the top-end talent underperforms simultaneously, this roster doesn't have enough reliable mid-tier scoring to compensate — which is why a lineup that looks this good on paper is sitting at 1-3.

teams.bio: No team swings wilder than the Mega Rats — Saccko bracket in 2022, league champions in 2023, a catastrophic 5-17 collapse in 2024, then a scorching 16-6 rebound in 2025 that ended in a championship runner-up finish. Caleb's team lives and dies by the hot hand, and the 2026 season so far is a perfect encapsulation of the Rats' identity: a 2-2 record that feels both promising and frustrating in equal measure, with Ronald Acuña Jr. still finding his footing.
teams.strengths: The Mega Rats have quietly built an impressive supporting cast around Acuña. Sal Stewart has been the team's best player through the early weeks, posting numbers that would be the headliner on most other rosters. Yandy Diaz is hitting at an elite level, and Yoshinobu Yamamoto, Dylan Cease, and Ranger Suarez give the Rats three legitimate rotation arms with real upside. When Acuña eventually gets going — and he will — this roster has the depth to become genuinely dangerous.
teams.weaknesses: Ronald Acuña Jr. has been a disappointment through the first month, posting modest numbers far below what this roster needs from its anchor. Garrett Crochet, another prized rotation piece, has underperformed his billing. The Mega Rats have just enough depth to stay around .500 when the stars aren't clicking, but the volatility that defines this franchise has already shown up in 2026 — and without Acuña leading the charge, the ceiling on this team stalls out somewhere in the middle of the standings.

teams.bio: The Emus own the league's best all-time record and have made the playoffs three times — yet a championship has remained elusive, which may be the most maddening story in the league. The secret weapon has always been Shohei Ohtani, who stands as the single most dominant player in Continental Breakfast Alliance history. In 2026, the elusive title may finally be within reach: Ben's squad is one of just two teams to start 4-0, and they're doing it while Ohtani is operating well below his ceiling.
teams.strengths: This team's depth is genuinely remarkable. Jordan Walker, CJ Abrams, Carlos Correa, Max Muncy, Riley Greene, and a deep outfield are all producing at high levels — the Emus are winning right now almost entirely without their superstar. When Shohei Ohtani returns to full two-way production, this roster becomes the most well-rounded in the league. Ben has also quietly assembled a bullpen that may be the deepest in the CBA, with Helsley, Erceg, Wrobleski, and Sewald all logging useful innings.
teams.weaknesses: Nick Lodolo and Spencer Schwellenbach — two starters this team was counting on — have both been zeros so far, leaving the back of the rotation vulnerable. That's a significant hole to paper over, and if the pitching depth gets tested further by injury, the Emus could find themselves relying on a rotation held together with wire and hope. There's also the lingering postseason question: three appearances without a ring suggests something about how this roster performs when the margin for error shrinks.

teams.bio: The Sky Chiefs have had a turbulent journey — a Saccko bracket finish in 2023 gave way to a breakout 2024 in which Michael's squad went 14-8 and posted one of the highest-scoring seasons in league history. Aaron Judge has been the franchise cornerstone, a perennial monster who makes every week a must-watch. In 2026, the supporting cast has arrived in force: Kevin McGonigle — the team's protected prospect — is already putting up starting numbers, and the Sky Chiefs sit at 3-1 looking every bit like a legitimate title threat.
teams.strengths: Aaron Judge is still the anchor, but the Sky Chiefs have quietly built one of the deepest rosters in the league. Brice Turang has been excellent, Kevin McGonigle has made an immediate impact after his call-up, and Chase DeLauter is posting star-level numbers. Sandy Alcantara has looked like an ace, and Mason Miller gives Michael one of the most dominant relievers in the game at the back of the bullpen. This team is deeper than it has ever been, and it's not even fully healthy yet.
teams.weaknesses: Gerrit Cole has not thrown a pitch in 2026, and his absence is a significant hole at the back of what was supposed to be an elite rotation. The Sky Chiefs also have an inherent volatility risk: when Judge has a down week — or an injury — the margin between a great week and a bad one narrows quickly. Alejandro Kirk has been nearly invisible behind the plate. If this roster underperforms its elite ceiling again in October, the window starts to feel finite.

teams.bio: In five seasons, the Whistlepigs have yet to make a single playoff appearance — the only team in the league with that distinction. Luke's squad has shown real improvement, including a 12-10 record in 2024, but a 2026 that has opened 0-4 is threatening to undo all of that progress. The brutal part is that this team isn't playing badly — the points are there — but the schedule has been merciless, and the Whistlepigs are running out of runway to prove this wasn't just another near-miss.
teams.strengths: There is genuine talent here that the record doesn't reflect. Nico Hoerner has been the team's best player and is having a standout early season. William Contreras, Ivan Herrera, and Nick Kurtz are all producing at legitimate starter levels, and Mitch Keller, Nick Martinez, and Michael Wacha form a rotation that is better than most of the league gives it credit for. The Whistlepigs are actually scoring at a decent clip — they've just had the misfortune of facing the league's best opponents in the first four weeks.
teams.weaknesses: A 0-4 start — however unlucky — creates a deep hole in a 21-week season. The Whistlepigs still lack a true franchise cornerstone, a player who can post 40-point weeks and change the math of a matchup by himself. Joe Musgrove is already out. The pitching staff is serviceable but not dominant, which means the margin for error against elite offenses disappears fast. Until Luke lands that top-of-the-draft-caliber anchor, this team will keep bumping up against its ceiling.

teams.bio: The Fuzzy Bottoms own the league's worst all-time record at 34-51, and the losses have piled up despite boasting two of the most exciting players in the league. The talent has always been undeniable — but talent alone hasn't been enough. In 2026, something is quietly different: Dugan has built a pitching staff that looks like the best in the CBA, and the team sits at 2-2 with a roster that, for the first time, doesn't live and die by its superstars.
teams.strengths: The pitching staff is the story of this team in 2026. Gavin Williams, Chris Sale, Landen Roupp, Jacob deGrom, and Shota Imanaga is a starting five that belongs in any conversation about the best rotation in the league. Five legitimate arms is an almost unheard-of luxury in keeper ball, and it gives Dugan a margin for error that this franchise has never had before. Alec Burleson has also emerged as a breakout bat, leading the team in points in the early going — proof that the supporting cast has finally arrived.
teams.weaknesses: Mookie Betts has barely played, logging just 31.5 points through the first five weeks — a combination of injury and early-season struggles that leaves the lineup thinner than it should be. The Fuzzy Bottoms' all-time record (34-51) is a reminder that this franchise has seen this movie before: great players, promising start, something goes sideways. The hitting depth outside of Witt and Burleson is still questionable, and if any two of those five starters hit the IL, the pitching advantage evaporates fast.

teams.bio: The Bristol Banshees arrived in 2025 as the league's newest franchise and immediately made everyone else look foolish — a 13-9 regular season record and a league championship in year one. Built around the generational talent of Juan Soto, Jacob's squad entered 2026 as defending champions and heavy favorites. The early returns have been humbling: a 1-3 start, a sluggish Soto, and a roster that looks like it's waiting for something to click.
teams.strengths: Tarik Skubal is the best pitcher in the league this season — nobody else is close. Corbin Carroll, Gunnar Henderson, and Roman Anthony give the Banshees three young cornerstone hitters who should be pillars of this franchise for years. Connelly Early, the team's protected prospect, has already made an impact on the mound. The championship infrastructure is still here, and one good week from Juan Soto could turn this team's season around entirely.
teams.weaknesses: Juan Soto has been the biggest disappointment of the 2026 season so far — 73 points from the supposed best player in the game is nowhere near what Jacob needs to compete. Spencer Strider hasn't thrown a pitch. The championship hangover is real: title rosters in keeper leagues often shed depth as other managers pick apart the winners in the offseason, and the Banshees are thinner than they were last October. How Jacob responds to being 1-3 as defending champions will define whether this becomes a dynasty or a one-year story.

teams.bio: The Folksy Ferrets have been the league's most quietly dangerous team — three playoff appearances, a 47-37 all-time record that ranks second in the league, and a 15-7 breakout in 2025 that had them looking like the favorite heading into 2026. The early season has been a brutal reality check: an 0-4 start and the league's lowest point total through four weeks. Daniel's team isn't just losing — it's struggling to score, and the injuries are already piling up.
teams.strengths: The pitching staff at the top remains genuinely elite. Jose Soriano has been the best pitcher in the league this season — 153 points through five weeks is an extraordinary number — and Tyler Glasnow has looked dominant alongside him. Kevin Gausman adds a legitimate third arm. When these three are rolling, the Ferrets can outscore anyone on the mound. The franchise's 47-37 all-time record is real; this team knows how to compete, and Daniel has shown before that he can right a sinking ship.
teams.weaknesses: The 2026 season is threatening to fall apart before it starts. Vladimir Guerrero Jr. is contributing at a solid but not star level, and the rest of the offense has been largely invisible — Kyle Tucker and Cody Bellinger are underperforming, Jeremy Pena has barely played, and Dylan Crews is a zero. Josh Hader hasn't thrown a pitch. Matthew Boyd and Luis Castillo have been ineffective. The Ferrets are a team whose identity in 2025 was a balanced roster firing on all cylinders — right now almost nothing is firing, and an 0-4 hole in a 21-week season requires an almost immediate turnaround to save the year.
